1: ʾōhēb mûsār ʾōhēb dāʿat — “whoever loves discipline (mûsār) loves knowledge (dāʿat).” The love of mûsār (corrective instruction) is the pragmatic test of whether someone genuinely wants knowledge. Śōnēʾ tôkaḥat baʿar (whoever hates correction is without understanding — lit. “is stupid/brutish”) — baʿar (H1197, brutish, like a dumb animal) is the strongest negative term in Proverbs’ vocabulary for intellectual deficiency.
2: ṭôb yāpîq rāṣôn mēʾet YHWH — “a good man obtains favor (rāṣôn) from YHWH.” Pûq rāṣôn (to bring forth favor) — pûq is to bring out a supply, suggesting favor is available and the good person draws on it. Wĕʾîš mĕzimmôt yarśîaʿ (a man who schemes wickedly he condemns) — YHWH’s condemnation is the decisive reversal of the schemer’s plans.
3: lōʾ-yikkôn ʾādām bĕrešaʿ — “no one is made secure by wickedness.” Kûn (to be established, secured, firm) in the niphal is the word for a stable foundation; wickedness cannot produce it. Wĕšōreš ṣaddîqîm bal-yimmôṭ (the root of the righteous will not be shaken) — the root (šōreš) image is organic and deep; the righteous person is grounded where wickedness cannot go.
4: ʾēšet ḥayil ʿăteret baʿlāh — “an excellent wife (ʾēšet ḥayil) is a crown to her husband.” Ḥayil (valor, strength, ability) in ʾēšet ḥayil is the same phrase used for the Woman of Valor in Proverbs 31:10 — the ideal of full competence and character. Ûkĕrāqāb bĕʿaṣmôtāyw (shame is like decay in his bones) — the contrast of crown-honor and bone-rot is deliberately visceral.
5: maḥšĕbôt ṣaddîqîm mišpāṭ — “the plans of the righteous are just (mišpāṭ).” Maḥšābet (plan, thought) as the interior level of moral action: the righteous person’s thinking itself is justice-shaped. The schemes (taḥbûlôt) of the wicked are deceitful (mirmāh) — the same word used for dishonest scales in Proverbs 11:1.
6: diḇrê rĕšāʿîm ʾĕrob-dām — “the words of the wicked set an ambush for blood (ʾĕrob-dām).” The wicked person’s speech is predatory — an ambush laid with words. Pî yĕšārîm yaṣṣîlēm (the mouth of the upright delivers people) — the same mouth-as-agent that ambushes in the wicked rescues in the upright.
7: hāpok rĕšāʿîm wĕʾênām — “the wicked are overthrown and are gone.” Hāpak (to overturn, be overthrown) is the verb of Sodom’s destruction (Genesis 19:29; Isaiah 13:19) — the wicked person’s end is a catastrophic overturning with no recovery. Bêt ṣaddîqîm yaʿămōd (the house of the righteous stands firm) — the household as the durable unit of righteous life.
8: lĕpî-śiklô yĕhullal ʾîš — “a man is praised according to his wisdom (śekel).” Śekel (prudence, good sense) is the practical-intelligence dimension of wisdom. Naʿăwē-lēb (twisted of mind/heart) is the antonym — structural deformation of the lēb that produces contempt (bāzāh) from others. Reputation tracks inner character.
9: ṭôb niqləh wĕʿebed lô — “it is better to be of humble standing and have a servant than to put on airs (mitkabbēd) and lack bread.” The proverb punctures pretension: self-aggrandizement (kābad in the hitpael — presenting oneself as honored) that is materially empty is worse than genuine but modest sufficiency. Reality outranks performance.
10: yôdēaʿ ṣaddîq nepeš bĕhemtô — “a righteous man cares for the wellbeing (nepeš) of his animal.” Nepeš applied to the animal — the righteous person attends to the animal’s vitality and appetite. Wĕraḥămê rĕšāʿîm ʾakzārî (even the compassion of the wicked is cruel) — the wicked person’s apparent mercy contains cruelty; character shapes every act.
11: ʿōbēd ʾadmātô yiśbaʿ lāḥem — “whoever works his land will have plenty of bread.” The agricultural work-ethic proverb: tilling the land (ʿābad ʾadāmāh) produces bread (śābaʿ lāḥem). Mĕraḥēp rêqîm ḥăsar-lēb (whoever chases empty pursuits lacks heart/sense) — rêqîm (empty things, vain pursuits) is the pursuit of what cannot sustain.
12: ḥāmad rāšāʿ mĕṣôd rāʿîm — “the wicked craves what evil people catch.” Ḥāmad (to desire covetously — used in the Decalogue, Exodus 20:17) applied to the wicked person’s desire for the prey of other evil people — coveting even the fruits of evil. Šōreš ṣaddîqîm yittēn (the root of the righteous produces its own fruit) — the righteous person generates their own fruitfulness, not dependent on predatory acquisition.
13: bĕpeša śĕpātayim môqēš rāʿ — “an evil man is trapped (môqēš, a snare) by the sins of his lips.” Speech as self-entrapment: the evil person’s own words form the snare. Wĕyēṣē miṣṣārāh ṣaddîq (the righteous escapes from trouble) — the juxtaposition of the wicked trapped by speech and the righteous escaping trouble highlights the moral asymmetry in crisis.
14: miprî pî-ʾîš yiśbaʿ-ṭôb — “from the fruit of his words a man is satisfied with good.” The mouth-as-fruit-tree image of 11:30 extended to the satisfaction (śābaʿ) of the speaker himself. Ûpĕʿullat yĕdê ʾādām tāšûb lô (the work of his hands comes back to him) — the reciprocity principle: both verbal and manual labor return their quality to the agent.
15: derek ʾĕwîl yāšār bĕʿênāyw — “the path of a fool seems right (yāšār) in his own eyes.” Self-perception as epistemological trap: the fool’s path is subjectively right because he lacks the external correction that exposes its error. Wĕšômēaʿ lāʿēṣāh ḥākām (a wise person listens to counsel) — receptivity to external correction is the distinguishing mark of wisdom over folly.
16: ʾĕwîl bayyôm yiwwādaʿ kaʿasô — “a fool’s annoyance (kaʿas) is known at once.” Kaʿas (vexation, anger, irritation) is immediately public when it belongs to the fool — no filter between inner state and outer expression. Wĕkōsē qālôn ʿārûm (a prudent person overlooks an insult) — kāsāh (to cover) is the same verb used of love covering offenses in Proverbs 10:12; the prudent person applies the same covering to minor social slights.
17: yāpîaḥ ʾĕmûnāh yaggîd ṣedeq — “a truthful witness (ʿēd ʾĕmet) gives honest testimony (ṣedeq).” ʾĂmûnāh (faithfulness, reliability) is the character quality behind truthful testimony; the true witness’s words are ṣedeq (right, just). Wĕʿēd šĕqārîm mirmāh (a false witness deceitfulness) — the false witness’s core is mirmāh (treachery, the dishonest-scales word of Proverbs 11:1).
18: yēš bôṭē dōbĕr kĕmadqĕrôt ḥāreb — “reckless words (bôṭē) pierce like sword-thrusts (madqĕrôt ḥāreb).” Bāṭāh (to speak rashly/thoughtlessly) creates word-weapons: the image of being stabbed by speech captures the visceral damage of careless talk. Wĕlāšôn ḥăkāmîm marpēʾ (the tongue of the wise brings healing — marpēʾ) — the same tongue-instrument that pierces in the reckless heals in the wise.
19: śĕpat ʾĕmet tikkôn lāʿad — “truthful lips stand firm (tikkôn) forever (lāʿad).” Kûn in the niphal (to be established, firm) applied to truthful lips: truth has ontological staying power — it is established in the order of things. Wĕʿad-ʾargeʿāh lĕšôn šāqer (a lying tongue lasts only a moment) — ʾargeʿāh (a moment, a wink of time) is the lifespan of the lie.
20: mirmāh bĕlēb ḥōrĕšê rāʿ — “deceit fills the heart of those who plan evil.” Ḥāraš (to plow, to devise) — the agricultural metaphor of plowing applied to devising evil; the heart-field is plowed with deceit. Wĕliôʿăṣê šālôm śimḥāh (those who counsel peace find joy) — šālôm as the goal of speech acts and its reward being śimḥāh (genuine joy).
21: lōʾ-yĕʾunneh laṣṣaddîq kol-ʾāwen — “no harm (ʾāwen) happens to the righteous.” ʾĀwen (trouble, wickedness, iniquity) in the sense of harm that befalls one. The proverb is experiential wisdom, not a metaphysical guarantee; its counterpart is that the wicked are filled with trouble (rāʿāh). The righteous person’s moral alignment creates a form of protection.
22: tôʿăbat YHWH śiprê šāqer — “lying lips are detestable (tôʿăbāh) to YHWH.” The tôʿăbāh-formula (applied to dishonest scales in Proverbs 11:1; to lying lips here) marks the most serious moral violations — those that are not merely unwise but abhorrent to divine character. Wĕʿōśê ʾĕmûnāh rĕṣônô (those who act faithfully are his delight) — ʾĕmûnāh as faithful reliability is the positive pole of YHWH’s pleasure.
23: ʾādām ʿārûm kōsē dāʿat — “a prudent man (ʿārûm) conceals what he knows.” ʿĀrûm (shrewd, prudent) in Proverbs is consistently positive (unlike Genesis 3:1 where the serpent is ʿārûm as cunning); the positive ʿārûm conceals knowledge strategically — not deception but disciplined restraint. Wĕlēb kĕsîlîm yiqrāʾ ʾiwwelet (the heart of a fool broadcasts its folly) — folly calls out, wisdom is silent.
24: yad ḥārûṣîm timšôl — “the diligent (ḥārûṣ, lit. sharp/incised, hence eager) hand will rule.” Diligence leads to authority; the lazy (rĕmiyyāh) will be put to forced labor (mas) — the reversal of the expected social position. Mas is tribute-labor, typically imposed on the conquered; the lazy person’s fate is the lowest social tier.
25: dĕʾāgāh bĕlēb-ʾîš yašḥennāh — “anxiety (dĕʾāgāh) in a man’s heart weighs him down.” Dĕʾāgāh (anxious care, sorrow) bends (šāḥāh) the heart; the remedy is a ṭôb-dābār (a good word) — a spoken encouragement that makes glad (yĕśamĕḥennāh). The proverb captures the therapeutic power of speech as direct pastoral medicine.
26: yātēr mērēʿēhû ṣaddîq — “the righteous man guides his neighbor well.” Yātēr (to lead, guide) from tûr (to explore, search out a path) — the righteous person scouts the path for others. Wĕderek rĕšāʿîm taṭʿēm (the way of the wicked leads them astray) — tāʿāh (to wander, go astray) as the destination of the wicked person’s path: leading followers into error.
27: lōʾ-yaḥărōk rĕmiyyāh ṣêdô — “the lazy man does not even bother to prepare his game.” Ḥārak (to roast/singe) — the lazy person captures prey but does not bother to cook it: slackness at every stage, not only in the initial effort. Wĕhôn-ʾādām yāqār ḥārûṣ (the wealth of a diligent man is a precious thing) — diligence makes ordinary wealth precious.
28: bĕʾōraḥ ṣĕdāqāh ḥayyîm — “in the path of righteousness is life.” The climactic statement of Proverbs 12 and a summary of the two-ways theme across Proverbs 10-12: righteousness, wisdom, and the path of life are the same road. Wĕderek nĕtîbāh ʾal-māwet — “and along that road there is no death.” ʾAl-māwet (no-death) is the fullest expression of the life promised to the wise; the road of righteousness is defined by the absence of the death-trajectory.
